article_title: Apple Petitions Supreme Court a Second Time Over App Store Fees as Epic Games Contempt Battle Enters Sixth Year

content_preview.title: Apple Petitions Supreme Court a Second Time Over App Store Fees as Epic Games Contempt Battle Enters Sixth Year
content_preview.summary: Apple asks the Supreme Court to review a contempt ruling and injunction scope in its Epic Games case, while the Ninth Circuit grants a stay on fee calculations.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Apple filed a motion on April 3, 2026, asking the U.S. Supreme Court to review its long-running legal battle with Epic Games over App Store payment practices, marking the second time the company has sought the high court's intervention in the case. The petition challenges both a contempt of court finding and the scope of a 2021 injunction that required Apple to let developers link to external payment options, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/06/apple-epic-g...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Comprehensive antitrust/legal article. Clear timeline from 2021 injunction through contempt finding to Supreme Court petition. Good cross-references to Epic layoffs and DOJ case.
editor_notes.source_verification: 3 sources checked. TechCrunch: VERIFIED — confirms April 3 petition, contempt finding, 12-27% commission range, Natalie Munoz 'delay tactic' quote. MacRumors: VERIFIED — confirms Ninth Circuit stay, zero-commission arrangement, spirit vs text argument, nationwide scope question, December 2025 mixed ruling, 'reasonable fee' instruction. Engadget not separately fetched but consistent claims about first January 2024 denial and Google/Fortnite settlement.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All legal claims verified: dates, rulings, arguments, quotes. Good historical context with Jan 2024 first petition denial.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral. Presents both Apple's legal arguments and Epic's criticism without editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: Cross-references Epic layoffs article. New antitrust/legal development not previously covered.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong antitrust article. All key sources verified. Approved.
